title: SPD/well-posedness for CG: K is only PSD, so A may be singular

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
CG and PCG require the system operator to be symmetric positive (semi)definite on the iteration subspace, and CG-uniqueness requires SPD. The operator is
$$A=(Z\otimes K)^TSS^T(Z\otimes K)+\lambda(I_r\otimes K).$$

**Gap.** $A$ is symmetric PSD, but $K$ is only assumed **PSD** (an RKHS Gram matrix), not PD. Then $A$ can be singular: any $\vecop(W)$ with $KW=0$ lies in $\ker(I_r\otimes K)$ and, since $(Z\otimes K)\vecop(W)=\vecop(KWZ^T)=0$ as well, lies in $\ker A$. Claiming '$A\succ0$' is therefore false in general.

**What must be proved (either branch):**
1. If $K\succ0$ (e.g. after a small ridge or because the kernel is strictly PD on the data), show $A\succeq\lambda(I_r\otimes K)\succ0$, hence CG is well-posed and converges to the unique solution.
2. If $K$ is genuinely singular, show the system is consistent and run CG on $\mathrm{range}(A)$ (equivalently, restrict $W$ to $\mathrm{range}(K)$, the RKHS quotient). Prove that the ALS update $A_k=KW$ is independent of the component of $W$ in $\ker K$, so the recovered factor is well-defined even though $W$ is not unique.

Without this step the applicability of CG to the stated (possibly singular) system is unjustified.
